preamble: The shift from the voiced pharyngeal fricative [ʕ] (Ayin) to the glottal stop [ʔ] (Aleph/Hamza) is a process of weakening or de-pharyngealization. This involves moving the point of constriction from the pharynx up to the glottis. It is a very common development in modern Semitic languages where pharyngeal sounds are often simplified or lost.

examples.note: The historical pharyngeal 'għ' has merged with the glottal stop or become silent, though it still affects neighboring vowels.
In rapid or urban speech, the 'ayin' can weaken to a glottal stop or a glottalized vowel.
Standard Israeli Hebrew largely merges Ayin with Aleph (glottal stop).

sources: Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Sáenz-Badillos, A. (1993). A History of the Hebrew Language.
